content.text: Union & Intersection Types

Union and intersection types are two fundamental ways to combine types in TypeScript. A union type (|) means a value can be one of several types. An intersection type (&) means a value must satisfy all of the combined types simultaneously.

attrs.alt: Union (A|B accepts either type) vs Intersection (A&B requires both types simultaneously) illustrated as Venn diagrams

content.text: Union Types (|)
A union type accepts a value that satisfies any one of its members. You write it with a pipe character between each member type.
Narrowing a Union
When a variable is typed as a union, TypeScript only allows operations that are valid for all members. To access member-specific features, you narrow the type with a type guard.
Discriminated Unions — The Recommended Pattern

A discriminated union is a union where every member has a common literal-typed property (the discriminant) that uniquely identifies the variant. This pattern enables exhaustive checks and clean narrowing.
Intersection Types (&)
An intersection type combines multiple types into one. A value of the intersection type must satisfy every member type simultaneously. It is the TypeScript equivalent of "implements all of these."
Intersection for Extending Existing Types
Intersection is useful when you want to add fields to an existing type without redefining it — especially when you don't own the original interface.
Conflict Resolution in Intersections

When the same property name appears in both sides of an intersection, the resulting type for that property is itself an intersection of the two individual types. If those types are incompatible, the property type becomes never.
Union vs Intersection — Key Differences
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Aspect'}
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Union  A | B'}
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Intersection  A & B'}
Meaning
Value is either A or B
Value is both A and B
Property access
Only shared properties (without narrowing)
All properties from all members
Width (number of values)
Wider — more values qualify
Narrower — fewer values qualify
Common use
Optional params, error/success states
Mixins, extending types, composition
Primitive combination
string | number
— either
string & number
→ never
Object combination
Shared properties accessible directly
All properties from all types required
Angular Patterns
Union for Component State
Intersection for Route Data and Guards
Utility Types Built on Unions and Intersections
Common Mistakes and Fixes
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Mistake'}
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Problem'}
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Fix'}
Accessing a member-specific property on a union without narrowing
TS2339 — property does not exist on all members
Narrow with typeof, in, or discriminant switch
Using intersection where you mean union
Type becomes impossibly narrow (or never for primitives)
Review semantics: | for "either", & for "both"
Intersecting two interfaces with conflicting property types
Property becomes never — no value can satisfy it
Rename the conflicting property or restructure the types
Forgetting assertNever in discriminated union switch
New variants silently fall through to undefined behaviour
Always add default: assertNever(x) to exhaustive switches
Using object spread (...) to "intersect" at runtime
Spread creates a new object — it is not the same as & at the type level
Type the result as A & B; use spread for the value; they stay in sync
Union Distribution in Conditional Types

When a union type is used as the checked type in a conditional type, TypeScript distributes the condition over each member individually. This is called distributive conditional types and is central to many utility types.
Template Literal Types with Unions
When a union is used inside a template literal type, TypeScript generates all combinations — the cross-product.
Recursive and Nested Union Types

Unions can be recursive — a type can reference itself — enabling tree-shaped or list-shaped structures. TypeScript supports this as long as each recursive reference is guarded behind an object property.
Intersection in Practice — Object Spread vs Type Intersection

At the type level, & creates an intersection type. At the value level, the object spread (...) achieves the same structural effect. Understanding the relationship helps you write code where types and values stay in sync.
TypeScript's
operator models structural intersection at the type level. Object spread (
) achieves the same shape at the value level for flat objects — but nested properties require a deep merge function, not just spread.
Summary
Union and intersection types let you compose types expressively without duplicating definitions:
Union (|):
"either A or B" — the value qualifies as one of the listed types; only shared properties are accessible without narrowing
Intersection (&):
"both A and B" — the value must satisfy every member type; all properties from all members are required and accessible
Discriminated unions:
add a common literal
kind
property to each variant; enables exhaustive switch + assertNever pattern
Narrowing:
use
typeof
instanceof
in
, or discriminant checks to unlock member-specific properties from a union
Intersection for composition:
combine interfaces instead of extending them; useful for mixin patterns and adding fields to third-party types
Angular:
discriminated unions model async component state (idle/loading/success/error); intersections compose service interfaces and route data shapes
